Math416Exam reviewsMath 416 Midterm 1 Review

Math 416 Midterm 1 Review

So everything we have learned so far is to extend the real line to the complex plane.

Chapter 0 Calculus on Real values

Differentiation

Let f,gf,gf,g be function on real line and ccc be a real number.

ddx(f+g)=f′+g′\frac{d}{dx}(f+g)=f'+g'dxd​(f+g)=f′+g′ ddx(cf)=cf′\frac{d}{dx}(cf)=cf'dxd​(cf)=cf′ ddx(fg)=f′g+fg′\frac{d}{dx}(fg)=f'g+fg'dxd​(fg)=f′g+fg′ ddx(f/g)=(f′g−fg′)/g2\frac{d}{dx}(f/g)=(f'g-fg')/g^2dxd​(f/g)=(f′g−fg′)/g2 ddx(f∘g)=(f′∘g)ddxg\frac{d}{dx}(f\circ g)=(f'\circ g)\frac{d}{dx}gdxd​(f∘g)=(f′∘g)dxd​g ddxxn=nxn−1\frac{d}{dx}x^n=nx^{n-1}dxd​xn=nxn−1 ddxex=ex\frac{d}{dx}e^x=e^xdxd​ex=ex ddxln⁡x=1x\frac{d}{dx}\ln x=\frac{1}{x}dxd​lnx=x1​ ddxsin⁡x=cos⁡x\frac{d}{dx}\sin x=\cos xdxd​sinx=cosx ddxcos⁡x=−sin⁡x\frac{d}{dx}\cos x=-\sin xdxd​cosx=−sinx ddxtan⁡x=sec⁡2x\frac{d}{dx}\tan x=\sec^2 xdxd​tanx=sec2x ddxsec⁡x=sec⁡xtan⁡x\frac{d}{dx}\sec x=\sec x\tan xdxd​secx=secxtanx ddxcsc⁡x=−csc⁡xcot⁡x\frac{d}{dx}\csc x=-\csc x\cot xdxd​cscx=−cscxcotx ddxsinh⁡x=cosh⁡x\frac{d}{dx}\sinh x=\cosh xdxd​sinhx=coshx ddxcosh⁡x=sinh⁡x\frac{d}{dx}\cosh x=\sinh xdxd​coshx=sinhx ddxtanh⁡x=sech⁡2x\frac{d}{dx}\tanh x=\operatorname{sech}^2 xdxd​tanhx=sech2x ddxsech⁡x=−sech⁡xtanh⁡x\frac{d}{dx}\operatorname{sech} x=-\operatorname{sech}x\tanh xdxd​sechx=−sechxtanhx ddxcsch⁡x=−csch⁡xcoth⁡x\frac{d}{dx}\operatorname{csch} x=-\operatorname{csch}x\coth xdxd​cschx=−cschxcothx ddxcoth⁡x=−csch⁡2x\frac{d}{dx}\coth x=-\operatorname{csch}^2 xdxd​cothx=−csch2x ddxarcsin⁡x=11−x2\frac{d}{dx}\arcsin x=\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}dxd​arcsinx=1−x2​1​ ddxarccos⁡x=−11−x2\frac{d}{dx}\arccos x=-\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}dxd​arccosx=−1−x2​1​ ddxarctan⁡x=11+x2\frac{d}{dx}\arctan x=\frac{1}{1+x^2}dxd​arctanx=1+x21​ ddxarccot⁡x=−11+x2\frac{d}{dx}\operatorname{arccot} x=-\frac{1}{1+x^2}dxd​arccotx=−1+x21​ ddxarcsec⁡x=1xx2−1\frac{d}{dx}\operatorname{arcsec} x=\frac{1}{x\sqrt{x^2-1}}dxd​arcsecx=xx2−1​1​ ddxarccsc⁡x=−1xx2−1\frac{d}{dx}\operatorname{arccsc} x=-\frac{1}{x\sqrt{x^2-1}}dxd​arccscx=−xx2−1​1​

Integration

Let f,gf,gf,g be function on real line and ccc be a real number.

∫(f+g)dx=∫fdx+∫gdx\int (f+g)dx=\int fdx+\int gdx∫(f+g)dx=∫fdx+∫gdx ∫cfdx=c∫fdx\int cfdx=c\int fdx∫cfdx=c∫fdx ∫exdx=ex\int e^x dx=e^x∫exdx=ex ∫ln⁡xdx=xln⁡x−x\int \ln x dx=x\ln x-x∫lnxdx=xlnx−x ∫1xdx=ln⁡∣x∣\int \frac{1}{x} dx=\ln|x|∫x1​dx=ln∣x∣ ∫sin⁡xdx=−cos⁡x\int \sin x dx=-\cos x∫sinxdx=−cosx ∫cos⁡xdx=sin⁡x\int \cos x dx=\sin x∫cosxdx=sinx ∫tan⁡xdx=−ln⁡∣cos⁡x∣\int \tan x dx=-\ln|\cos x|∫tanxdx=−ln∣cosx∣ ∫cot⁡xdx=ln⁡∣sin⁡x∣\int \cot x dx=\ln|\sin x|∫cotxdx=ln∣sinx∣ ∫sec⁡xdx=ln⁡∣sec⁡x+tan⁡x∣\int \sec x dx=\ln|\sec x+\tan x|∫secxdx=ln∣secx+tanx∣ ∫csc⁡xdx=ln⁡∣csc⁡x−cot⁡x∣\int \csc x dx=\ln|\csc x-\cot x|∫cscxdx=ln∣cscx−cotx∣ ∫sinh⁡xdx=cosh⁡x\int \sinh x dx=\cosh x∫sinhxdx=coshx ∫cosh⁡xdx=sinh⁡x\int \cosh x dx=\sinh x∫coshxdx=sinhx ∫tanh⁡xdx=ln⁡∣cosh⁡x∣\int \tanh x dx=\ln|\cosh x|∫tanhxdx=ln∣coshx∣ ∫coth⁡xdx=ln⁡∣sinh⁡x∣\int \coth x dx=\ln|\sinh x|∫cothxdx=ln∣sinhx∣ ∫sech⁡xdx=2arctan⁡(tanh⁡(x/2))\int \operatorname{sech} x dx=2\arctan(\tanh(x/2))∫sechxdx=2arctan(tanh(x/2)) ∫csch⁡xdx=ln⁡∣coth⁡x−csch⁡x∣\int \operatorname{csch} x dx=\ln|\coth x-\operatorname{csch} x|∫cschxdx=ln∣cothx−cschx∣ ∫sech⁡2xdx=tanh⁡x\int \operatorname{sech}^2 x dx=\tanh x∫sech2xdx=tanhx ∫csch⁡2xdx=−coth⁡x\int \operatorname{csch}^2 x dx=-\coth x∫csch2xdx=−cothx ∫11+x2dx=arctan⁡x\int \frac{1}{1+x^2} dx=\arctan x∫1+x21​dx=arctanx ∫1x2+1dx=arctan⁡x\int \frac{1}{x^2+1} dx=\arctan x∫x2+11​dx=arctanx ∫1x2−1dx=12ln⁡∣x−1x+1∣\int \frac{1}{x^2-1} dx=\frac{1}{2}\ln|\frac{x-1}{x+1}|∫x2−11​dx=21​ln∣x+1x−1​∣ ∫1x2−a2dx=12aln⁡∣x−ax+a∣\int \frac{1}{x^2-a^2} dx=\frac{1}{2a}\ln|\frac{x-a}{x+a}|∫x2−a21​dx=2a1​ln∣x+ax−a​∣ ∫1x2+a2dx=1aarctan⁡(xa)\int \frac{1}{x^2+a^2} dx=\frac{1}{a}\arctan(\frac{x}{a})∫x2+a21​dx=a1​arctan(ax​) ∫1x2−a2dx=ln⁡∣x+x2−a2∣\int \frac{1}{\sqrt{x^2-a^2}} dx=\ln|x+\sqrt{x^2-a^2}|∫x2−a2​1​dx=ln∣x+x2−a2​∣ ∫1x2+a2dx=ln⁡∣x+x2+a2∣\int \frac{1}{\sqrt{x^2+a^2}} dx=\ln|x+\sqrt{x^2+a^2}|∫x2+a2​1​dx=ln∣x+x2+a2​∣

Chapter 1 Complex Numbers

Definition of complex numbers

An ordered pair of real numbers (x,y)(x, y)(x,y) can be represented as a complex number z=x+yiz = x + yiz=x+yi, where iii is the imaginary unit.

With operations defined as:

(x1+y1i)+(x2+y2i)=(x1+x2)+(y1+y2)i(x_1 + y_1i) + (x_2 + y_2i) = (x_1 + x_2) + (y_1 + y_2)i(x1​+y1​i)+(x2​+y2​i)=(x1​+x2​)+(y1​+y2​)i (x1+y1i)⋅(x2+y2i)=(x1x2−y1y2)+(x1y2+x2y1)i(x_1 + y_1i) \cdot (x_2 + y_2i) = (x_1x_2 - y_1y_2) + (x_1y_2 + x_2y_1)i(x1​+y1​i)⋅(x2​+y2​i)=(x1​x2​−y1​y2​)+(x1​y2​+x2​y1​)i

Modulus

The modulus of a complex number z=x+yiz = x + yiz=x+yi is defined as

∣z∣=x2+y2=∣zz‾∣|z| = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}=|z\overline{z}|∣z∣=x2+y2​=∣zz∣

De Moivre’s Formula

Every complex number zzz can be written as z=r(cos⁡θ+isin⁡θ)z = r(\cos \theta + i \sin \theta)z=r(cosθ+isinθ), where rrr is the magnitude of zzz and θ\thetaθ is the argument of zzz.

zn=rn(cos⁡nθ+isin⁡nθ)z^n = r^n(\cos n\theta + i \sin n\theta)zn=rn(cosnθ+isinnθ)

The De Moivre’s formula is useful for finding the nnnth roots of a complex number.

zn=rn(cos⁡nθ+isin⁡nθ)z^n = r^n(\cos n\theta + i \sin n\theta)zn=rn(cosnθ+isinnθ)

Roots of complex numbers

Using De Moivre’s formula, we can find the nnnth roots of a complex number.

If z=r(cos⁡θ+isin⁡θ)z=r(\cos \theta + i \sin \theta)z=r(cosθ+isinθ), then the nnnth roots of zzz are given by:

zk=r1/n(cos⁡θ+2kπn+isin⁡θ+2kπn)z_k = r^{1/n}(\cos \frac{\theta + 2k\pi}{n} + i \sin \frac{\theta + 2k\pi}{n})zk​=r1/n(cosnθ+2kπ​+isinnθ+2kπ​)

for k=0,1,2,…,n−1k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, n-1k=0,1,2,…,n−1.

Stereographic projection

The stereographic projection is a map from the unit sphere S2S^2S2 to the complex plane C∖{0}\mathbb{C}\setminus\{0\}C∖{0}.

The projection is given by:

z↦(2Re(z),2Im(z),∣z∣2−1)∣z∣2+1z\mapsto \frac{(2Re(z), 2Im(z), |z|^2-1)}{|z|^2+1}z↦∣z∣2+1(2Re(z),2Im(z),∣z∣2−1)​

The inverse map is given by:

(ξ,η,ζ)↦ξ+iη1−ζ(\xi,\eta, \zeta)\mapsto \frac{\xi + i\eta}{1 - \zeta}(ξ,η,ζ)↦1−ζξ+iη​

Chapter 2 Complex Differentiation

Definition of complex differentiation

Let the complex plane C\mathbb{C}C be defined in an open subset GGG of C\mathbb{C}C. (Domain)

Then fff is said to be differentiable at z0∈Gz_0\in Gz0​∈G if the limit

lim⁡z→z0f(z)−f(z0)z−z0\lim_{z\to z_0} \frac{f(z)-f(z_0)}{z-z_0}z→z0​lim​z−z0​f(z)−f(z0​)​

exists.

The limit is called the derivative of fff at z0z_0z0​ and is denoted by f′(z0)f'(z_0)f′(z0​).

To prove that a function is differentiable, we can use the standard delta-epsilon definition of a limit.

∣f(z)−f(z0)z−z0−f′(z0)∣<ϵ\left|\frac{f(z)-f(z_0)}{z-z_0} - f'(z_0)\right| < \epsilon​z−z0​f(z)−f(z0​)​−f′(z0​)​<ϵ

whenever 0<∣z−z0∣<δ0 < |z-z_0| < \delta0<∣z−z0​∣<δ.

With such definition, all the properties of real differentiation can be extended to complex differentiation.

Differentiation of complex functions

  1. If fff is differentiable at z0z_0z0​, then fff is continuous at z0z_0z0​.
  2. If f,gf,gf,g are differentiable at z0z_0z0​, then f+g,fgf+g, fgf+g,fg are differentiable at z0z_0z0​. (f+g)′(z0)=f′(z0)+g′(z0)(f+g)'(z_0) = f'(z_0) + g'(z_0)(f+g)′(z0​)=f′(z0​)+g′(z0​) (fg)′(z0)=f′(z0)g(z0)+f(z0)g′(z0)(fg)'(z_0) = f'(z_0)g(z_0) + f(z_0)g'(z_0)(fg)′(z0​)=f′(z0​)g(z0​)+f(z0​)g′(z0​)
  3. If f,gf,gf,g are differentiable at z0z_0z0​ and g(z0)≠0g(z_0)\neq 0g(z0​)=0, then f/gf/gf/g is differentiable at z0z_0z0​. (fg)′(z0)=f′(z0)g(z0)−f(z0)g′(z0)g(z0)2\left(\frac{f}{g}\right)'(z_0) = \frac{f'(z_0)g(z_0) - f(z_0)g'(z_0)}{g(z_0)^2}(gf​)′(z0​)=g(z0​)2f′(z0​)g(z0​)−f(z0​)g′(z0​)​
  4. If fff is differentiable at z0z_0z0​ and ggg is differentiable at f(z0)f(z_0)f(z0​), then g∘fg\circ fg∘f is differentiable at z0z_0z0​. (g∘f)′(z0)=g′(f(z0))f′(z0)(g\circ f)'(z_0) = g'(f(z_0))f'(z_0)(g∘f)′(z0​)=g′(f(z0​))f′(z0​)
  5. If f(z)=∑k=0nck(z−z0)kf(z)=\sum_{k=0}^n c_k(z-z_0)^kf(z)=∑k=0n​ck​(z−z0​)k, where ck∈Cc_k\in\mathbb{C}ck​∈C, then fff is differentiable at z0z_0z0​ and f′(z0)=∑k=1nkck(z0−z0)k−1f'(z_0)=\sum_{k=1}^n kc_k(z_0-z_0)^{k-1}f′(z0​)=∑k=1n​kck​(z0​−z0​)k−1. f′(z0)=c1+2c2(z0−z0)+3c3(z0−z0)2+⋯+ncn(z0−z0)n−1f'(z_0) = c_1 + 2c_2(z_0-z_0) + 3c_3(z_0-z_0)^2 + \cdots + nc_n(z_0-z_0)^{n-1}f′(z0​)=c1​+2c2​(z0​−z0​)+3c3​(z0​−z0​)2+⋯+ncn​(z0​−z0​)n−1

Cauchy-Riemann Equations

Let the function defined on an open subset GGG of C\mathbb{C}C be f(x,y)=u(x,y)+iv(x,y)f(x,y)=u(x,y)+iv(x,y)f(x,y)=u(x,y)+iv(x,y), where u,vu,vu,v are real-valued functions.

Then fff is differentiable at z0=x0+y0iz_0=x_0+y_0iz0​=x0​+y0​i if and only if the partial derivatives of uuu and vvv exist at (x0,y0)(x_0,y_0)(x0​,y0​) and satisfy the Cauchy-Riemann equations:

∂u∂x=∂v∂y,∂u∂y=−∂v∂x\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial v}{\partial y}, \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = -\frac{\partial v}{\partial x}∂x∂u​=∂y∂v​,∂y∂u​=−∂x∂v​

On the polar form, the Cauchy-Riemann equations are

r∂u∂r=∂v∂θ,∂u∂θ=−r∂v∂rr\frac{\partial u}{\partial r} = \frac{\partial v}{\partial \theta}, \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial \theta} = -r\frac{\partial v}{\partial r}r∂r∂u​=∂θ∂v​,∂θ∂u​=−r∂r∂v​

Holomorphic functions

A function fff is said to be holomorphic on an open subset GGG of C\mathbb{C}C if fff is differentiable at every point of GGG.

Partial differential operators

∂∂z=12(∂∂x−i∂∂y)\frac{\partial}{\partial z} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial}{\partial x} - i\frac{\partial}{\partial y}\right)∂z∂​=21​(∂x∂​−i∂y∂​) ∂∂zˉ=12(∂∂x+i∂∂y)\frac{\partial}{\partial \bar{z}} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial}{\partial x} + i\frac{\partial}{\partial y}\right)∂zˉ∂​=21​(∂x∂​+i∂y∂​)

This gives that

∂f∂z=12(∂f∂x−i∂f∂y)=12(∂u∂x+∂v∂y)+i2(∂v∂x−∂u∂y)\frac{\partial f}{\partial z} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial x} - i\frac{\partial f}{\partial y}\right)=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} +\frac{\partial v}{\partial y}\right) + \frac{i}{2}\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right)∂z∂f​=21​(∂x∂f​−i∂y∂f​)=21​(∂x∂u​+∂y∂v​)+2i​(∂x∂v​−∂y∂u​) ∂f∂zˉ=12(∂f∂x+i∂f∂y)=12(∂u∂x−∂v∂y)+i2(∂u∂y+∂v∂x)\frac{\partial f}{\partial \bar{z}} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial x} + i\frac{\partial f}{\partial y}\right)=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial v}{\partial y}\right) + \frac{i}{2}\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}\right)∂zˉ∂f​=21​(∂x∂f​+i∂y∂f​)=21​(∂x∂u​−∂y∂v​)+2i​(∂y∂u​+∂x∂v​)

If the function fff is holomorphic, then by the Cauchy-Riemann equations, we have

∂f∂zˉ=0\frac{\partial f}{\partial \bar{z}} = 0∂zˉ∂f​=0

Conformal mappings

A holomorphic function fff is said to be conformal if it preserves the angles between the curves. More formally, if fff is holomorphic on an open subset GGG of C\mathbb{C}C and z0∈Gz_0\in Gz0​∈G, γ1,γ2\gamma_1, \gamma_2γ1​,γ2​ are two curves passing through z0z_0z0​ (γ1(t1)=γ2(t2)=z0\gamma_1(t_1)=\gamma_2(t_2)=z_0γ1​(t1​)=γ2​(t2​)=z0​) and intersecting at an angle θ\thetaθ, then

arg⁡(f∘γ1)′(t1)−arg⁡(f∘γ2)′(t2)=θ\arg(f\circ\gamma_1)'(t_1) - \arg(f\circ\gamma_2)'(t_2) = \thetaarg(f∘γ1​)′(t1​)−arg(f∘γ2​)′(t2​)=θ

In other words, the angle between the curves is preserved.

An immediate consequence is that

arg⁡(f⋅γ1)′(t1)=arg⁡f′(z0)+arg⁡γ1′(t1)arg⁡(f⋅γ2)′(t2)=arg⁡f′(z0)+arg⁡γ2′(t2)\arg(f\cdot \gamma_1)'(t_1) =\arg f'(z_0) + \arg \gamma_1'(t_1)\\ \arg(f\cdot \gamma_2)'(t_2) =\arg f'(z_0) + \arg \gamma_2'(t_2)arg(f⋅γ1​)′(t1​)=argf′(z0​)+argγ1′​(t1​)arg(f⋅γ2​)′(t2​)=argf′(z0​)+argγ2′​(t2​)

Harmonic functions

A real-valued function uuu is said to be harmonic if it satisfies the Laplace equation:

∂2u∂x2+∂2u∂y2=0\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2} = 0∂x2∂2u​+∂y2∂2u​=0

Chapter 3 Linear Fractional Transformations

Definition of linear fractional transformations

A linear fractional transformation is a function of the form

ϕ(z)=az+bcz+d\phi(z) = \frac{az+b}{cz+d}ϕ(z)=cz+daz+b​

where a,b,c,da,b,c,da,b,c,d are complex numbers and ad−bc≠0ad-bc\neq 0ad−bc=0.

Properties of linear fractional transformations

Matrix form

A linear fractional transformation can be written as a matrix multiplication:

ϕ(z)=[abcd][z1]\phi(z) = \begin{bmatrix} a & b\\ c & d\\ \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} z\\ 1\\ \end{bmatrix}ϕ(z)=[ac​bd​][z1​]

Conformality

A linear fractional transformation is conformal.

ϕ′(z)=ad−bc(cz+d)2\phi'(z) = \frac{ad-bc}{(cz+d)^2}ϕ′(z)=(cz+d)2ad−bc​

Three-fold transitivity

If z1,z2,z3z_1,z_2,z_3z1​,z2​,z3​ are distinct points in the complex plane, then there exists a unique linear fractional transformation ϕ\phiϕ such that ϕ(z1)=∞\phi(z_1)=\inftyϕ(z1​)=∞, ϕ(z2)=0\phi(z_2)=0ϕ(z2​)=0, ϕ(z3)=1\phi(z_3)=1ϕ(z3​)=1.

The map is given by

ϕ(z)={(z−z2)(z1−z3)(z−z1)(z2−z3)if z1,z2,z3 are all finitez−z2z3−z2if z1=∞z3−z1z−z1if z2=∞z−z2z−z1if z3=∞\phi(z) =\begin{cases} \frac{(z-z_2)(z_1-z_3)}{(z-z_1)(z_2-z_3)} & \text{if } z_1,z_2,z_3 \text{ are all finite}\\ \frac{z-z_2}{z_3-z_2} & \text{if } z_1=\infty\\ \frac{z_3-z_1}{z-z_1} & \text{if } z_2=\infty\\ \frac{z-z_2}{z-z_1} & \text{if } z_3=\infty\\ \end{cases}ϕ(z)=⎩⎨⎧​(z−z1​)(z2​−z3​)(z−z2​)(z1​−z3​)​z3​−z2​z−z2​​z−z1​z3​−z1​​z−z1​z−z2​​​if z1​,z2​,z3​ are all finiteif z1​=∞if z2​=∞if z3​=∞​

So if z1,z2,z3z_1,z_2,z_3z1​,z2​,z3​, w1,w2,w3w_1,w_2,w_3w1​,w2​,w3​ are distinct points in the complex plane, then there exists a unique linear fractional transformation ϕ\phiϕ such that ϕ(zi)=wi\phi(z_i)=w_iϕ(zi​)=wi​ for i=1,2,3i=1,2,3i=1,2,3.

Factorization

Every linear fractional transformation can be written as a composition of homothetic mappings, translations, inversions, and multiplications.

If ϕ(z)=az+bcz+d\phi(z)=\frac{az+b}{cz+d}ϕ(z)=cz+daz+b​, then

ϕ(z)=b−ad/ccz+d+ac\phi(z) = \frac{b-ad/c}{cz+d}+\frac{a}{c}ϕ(z)=cz+db−ad/c​+ca​

Clircle

A linear-fractional transformation maps circles and lines to circles and lines.

Chapter 4 Elementary Functions

Exponential function

The exponential function is defined as

ez=∑n=0∞znn!e^z = \sum_{n=0}^\infty \frac{z^n}{n!}ez=n=0∑∞​n!zn​

Let z=x+iyz=x+iyz=x+iy, then

ez=ex+iy=exeiy=ex∑n=0∞(iy)nn!=ex∑n=0∞(−1)ny2n(2n)!+i∑n=0∞(−1)ny2n+1(2n+1)!=ex(cos⁡y+isin⁡y)\begin{aligned} e^z &= e^{x+iy}\\ &= e^x e^{iy}\\ &= e^x\sum_{n=0}^\infty \frac{(iy)^n}{n!}\\ &= e^x\sum_{n=0}^\infty \frac{(-1)^n y^{2n}}{(2n)!} + i \sum_{n=0}^\infty \frac{(-1)^n y^{2n+1}}{(2n+1)!}\\ &= e^x(\cos y + i\sin y)\\ \end{aligned}ez​=ex+iy=exeiy=exn=0∑∞​n!(iy)n​=exn=0∑∞​(2n)!(−1)ny2n​+in=0∑∞​(2n+1)!(−1)ny2n+1​=ex(cosy+isiny)​

So we can rewrite the polar form of a complex number as

z=r(cos⁡θ+isin⁡θ)=reiθz = r(\cos \theta + i\sin \theta) = re^{i\theta}z=r(cosθ+isinθ)=reiθ

exe^xex is holomorphic

Let f(z)=ezf(z)=e^zf(z)=ez, then u(x,y)=excos⁡yu(x,y)=e^x\cos yu(x,y)=excosy, v(x,y)=exsin⁡yv(x,y)=e^x\sin yv(x,y)=exsiny.

∂u∂x=excos⁡y=∂v∂y∂u∂y=−exsin⁡y=−∂v∂x\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = e^x\cos y = \frac{\partial v}{\partial y}\\ \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = -e^x\sin y = -\frac{\partial v}{\partial x}∂x∂u​=excosy=∂y∂v​∂y∂u​=−exsiny=−∂x∂v​

Trigonometric functions

sin⁡z=eiz−e−iz2i,cos⁡z=eiz+e−iz2,tan⁡z=sin⁡zcos⁡z\sin z = \frac{e^{iz}-e^{-iz}}{2i}, \quad \cos z = \frac{e^{iz}+e^{-iz}}{2}, \quad \tan z = \frac{\sin z}{\cos z}sinz=2ieiz−e−iz​,cosz=2eiz+e−iz​,tanz=coszsinz​ sec⁡z=1cos⁡z,csc⁡z=1sin⁡z,cot⁡z=1tan⁡z\sec z = \frac{1}{\cos z}, \quad \csc z = \frac{1}{\sin z}, \quad \cot z = \frac{1}{\tan z}secz=cosz1​,cscz=sinz1​,cotz=tanz1​

Hyperbolic functions

sinh⁡z=ez−e−z2,cosh⁡z=ez+e−z2,tanh⁡z=sinh⁡zcosh⁡z\sinh z = \frac{e^z-e^{-z}}{2}, \quad \cosh z = \frac{e^z+e^{-z}}{2}, \quad \tanh z = \frac{\sinh z}{\cosh z}sinhz=2ez−e−z​,coshz=2ez+e−z​,tanhz=coshzsinhz​ sech⁡z=1cosh⁡z,csch⁡z=1sinh⁡z,coth⁡z=1tanh⁡z\operatorname{sech} z = \frac{1}{\cosh z}, \quad \operatorname{csch} z = \frac{1}{\sinh z}, \quad \operatorname{coth} z = \frac{1}{\tanh z}sechz=coshz1​,cschz=sinhz1​,cothz=tanhz1​

Logarithmic function

The logarithmic function is defined as

ln⁡z={w∈C:ew=z}\ln z=\{w\in\mathbb{C}: e^w=z\}lnz={w∈C:ew=z}

Properties of the logarithmic function

Let z=x+iyz=x+iyz=x+iy, then

∣ez∣=ex(cos⁡y)2+(sin⁡y)2=ex|e^z|=\sqrt{e^x(\cos y)^2+(\sin y)^2}=e^x∣ez∣=ex(cosy)2+(siny)2​=ex

So we have

log⁡z=ln⁡∣z∣+iarg⁡z\log z = \ln |z| + i\arg zlogz=ln∣z∣+iargz

Power function

For any two complex numbers a,ba,ba,b, we can define the power function as

ab=eblog⁡aa^b = e^{b\log a}ab=ebloga

Example:

ii=eiln⁡i=ei(ln⁡1+iπ2)=e−π2i^i=e^{i\ln i}=e^{i(\ln 1+i\frac{\pi}{2})}=e^{-\frac{\pi}{2}} ii=eilni=ei(ln1+i2π​)=e−2π​

eiπ=−1e^{i\pi}=-1eiπ=−1

Chapter 5 Power Series

Definition of power series

A power series is a series of the form

∑n=0∞an(z−z0)n\sum_{n=0}^\infty a_n (z-z_0)^nn=0∑∞​an​(z−z0​)n

Properties of power series

Geometric series

∑n=0∞zn=11−z,∣z∣<1\sum_{n=0}^\infty z^n = \frac{1}{1-z}, \quad |z|<1n=0∑∞​zn=1−z1​,∣z∣<1

Radius/Region of convergence

The radius of convergence of a power series is the largest number RRR such that the series converges for all zzz with ∣z−z0∣<R|z-z_0|<R∣z−z0​∣<R.

The region of convergence of a power series is the set of all points zzz such that the series converges.

Cauchy-Hadamard Theorem

The radius of convergence of a power series is given by

R=1lim sup⁡n→∞∣an∣1/nR=\frac{1}{\limsup_{n\to\infty} |a_n|^{1/n}}R=limsupn→∞​∣an​∣1/n1​

Derivative of power series

The derivative of a power series is given by

f′(z)=∑n=1∞nan(z−z0)n−1f'(z)=\sum_{n=1}^\infty n a_n (z-z_0)^{n-1}f′(z)=n=1∑∞​nan​(z−z0​)n−1

Cauchy Product (of power series)

Let ∑n=0∞an(z−z0)n\sum_{n=0}^\infty a_n (z-z_0)^n∑n=0∞​an​(z−z0​)n and ∑n=0∞bn(z−z0)n\sum_{n=0}^\infty b_n (z-z_0)^n∑n=0∞​bn​(z−z0​)n be two power series with radius of convergence R1R_1R1​ and R2R_2R2​ respectively.

Then the Cauchy product of the two series is given by

∑n=0∞cn(z−z0)n\sum_{n=0}^\infty c_n (z-z_0)^nn=0∑∞​cn​(z−z0​)n

where

cn=∑k=0nakbn−kc_n = \sum_{k=0}^n a_k b_{n-k}cn​=k=0∑n​ak​bn−k​

The radius of convergence of the Cauchy product is at least min⁡(R1,R2)\min(R_1,R_2)min(R1​,R2​).

Chapter 6 Complex Integration

Definition of Riemann Integral for complex functions

The complex integral of a complex function ϕ\phiϕ on the closed subinterval [a,b][a,b][a,b] of the real line is said to be piecewise continuous if there exists a partition a=t0<t1<⋯<tn=ba=t_0<t_1<\cdots<t_n=ba=t0​<t1​<⋯<tn​=b such that ϕ\phiϕ is continuous on each open interval (ti−1,ti)(t_{i-1},t_i)(ti−1​,ti​) and has a finite limit at each discontinuity point of the closed interval [a,b][a,b][a,b].

If ϕ\phiϕ is piecewise continuous on [a,b][a,b][a,b], then the complex integral of ϕ\phiϕ on [a,b][a,b][a,b] is defined as

∫abϕ(t)dt=∫abRe⁡ϕ(t)dt+i∫abIm⁡ϕ(t)dt\int_a^b \phi(t) dt = \int_a^b \operatorname{Re}\phi(t) dt + i\int_a^b \operatorname{Im}\phi(t) dt∫ab​ϕ(t)dt=∫ab​Reϕ(t)dt+i∫ab​Imϕ(t)dt

Fundamental Theorem of Calculus

If ϕ\phiϕ is piecewise continuous on [a,b][a,b][a,b], then

∫abϕ′(t)dt=ϕ(b)−ϕ(a)\int_a^b \phi'(t) dt = \phi(b)-\phi(a)∫ab​ϕ′(t)dt=ϕ(b)−ϕ(a)

Triangle inequality

∣∫abϕ(t)dt∣≤∫ab∣ϕ(t)∣dt\left|\int_a^b \phi(t) dt\right| \leq \int_a^b |\phi(t)| dt​∫ab​ϕ(t)dt​≤∫ab​∣ϕ(t)∣dt

Integral on curve

Let γ\gammaγ be a piecewise smooth curve in the complex plane.

The integral of a complex function fff on γ\gammaγ is defined as

∫γf(z)dz=∫abf(γ(t))γ′(t)dt\int_\gamma f(z) dz = \int_a^b f(\gamma(t))\gamma'(t) dt∫γ​f(z)dz=∫ab​f(γ(t))γ′(t)dt

Favorite estimate

Let γ:[a,b]→C\gamma:[a,b]\to\mathbb{C}γ:[a,b]→C be a piecewise smooth curve, and let f:[a,b]→Cf:[a,b]\to\mathbb{C}f:[a,b]→C be a continuous complex-valued function. Let MMM be a real number such that ∣f(z)∣≤M|f(z)|\leq M∣f(z)∣≤M for all z∈γz\in\gammaz∈γ. Then

∣∫γf(z)dz∣≤Mℓ(γ)\left|\int_\gamma f(z) dz\right| \leq M\ell(\gamma)​∫γ​f(z)dz​≤Mℓ(γ)

where ℓ(γ)\ell(\gamma)ℓ(γ) is the length of the curve γ\gammaγ.

Chapter 7 Cauchy’s Theorem

Cauchy’s Theorem

Let γ\gammaγ be a closed curve in C\mathbb{C}C and UUU be a simply connected open subset of C\mathbb{C}C containing γ\gammaγ and its interior. Let fff be a holomorphic function on UUU. Then

∫γf(z)dz=0\int_\gamma f(z) dz = 0∫γ​f(z)dz=0

Cauchy’s Formula for a Circle

Let CCC be a counterclockwise oriented circle and let fff be holomorphic function defined in an open set containing CCC and its interior. Then for any zzz in the interior of CCC,

f(z)=12πi∫Cf(ζ)ζ−zdζf(z)=\frac{1}{2\pi i}\int_C \frac{f(\zeta)}{\zeta-z} d\zetaf(z)=2πi1​∫C​ζ−zf(ζ)​dζ

Mean Value Property

Let the function fff be holomorphic on a disk ∣z−z0∣<R|z-z_0|<R∣z−z0​∣<R. Then for any 0<r<R0<r<R0<r<R, let CrC_rCr​ denote the circle with center z0z_0z0​ and radius rrr. Then

f(z0)=12π∫02πf(z0+reiθ)dθf(z_0)=\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_0^{2\pi} f(z_0+re^{i\theta}) d\thetaf(z0​)=2π1​∫02π​f(z0​+reiθ)dθ

The value of the function at the center of the disk is the average of the values of the function on the boundary of the disk.

Cauchy Integrals

Let γ\gammaγ be a piecewise smooth curve in C\mathbb{C}C and let ϕ\phiϕ be a continuous complex-valued function on γ\gammaγ. Then the Cauchy integral of ϕ\phiϕ on γ\gammaγ is the function fff defined in C∖γC\setminus\gammaC∖γ by

f(z)=∫γϕ(ζ)ζ−zdζf(z)=\int_\gamma \frac{\phi(\zeta)}{\zeta-z} d\zetaf(z)=∫γ​ζ−zϕ(ζ)​dζ

Cauchy Integral Formula for circle CrC_rCr​:

f(z)=12πi∫Crf(ζ)ζ−zdζf(z)=\frac{1}{2\pi i}\int_{C_r} \frac{f(\zeta)}{\zeta-z} d\zetaf(z)=2πi1​∫Cr​​ζ−zf(ζ)​dζ

Example:

Evaluate ∫∣z∣=2zz−1dz\int_{|z|=2} \frac{z}{z-1} dz∫∣z∣=2​z−1z​dz

Note that if we let f(ζ)=ζf(\zeta)=\zetaf(ζ)=ζ and z=1z=1z=1 is inside the circle, then we can use Cauchy Integral Formula for circle CrC_rCr​ to evaluate the integral.

So we have

∫∣z∣=2zz−1dz=2πif(1)=2πi\int_{|z|=2} \frac{z}{z-1} dz = 2\pi i f(1) = 2\pi i∫∣z∣=2​z−1z​dz=2πif(1)=2πi

General Cauchy Integral Formula for circle CrC_rCr​:

f(n)(z)=n!2πi∫Crf(ζ)(ζ−z)n+1dζf^{(n)}(z)=\frac{n!}{2\pi i}\int_{C_r} \frac{f(\zeta)}{(\zeta-z)^{n+1}} d\zetaf(n)(z)=2πin!​∫Cr​​(ζ−z)n+1f(ζ)​dζ

Example:

Evaluate ∫Csin⁡zz38dz\int_{C}\frac{\sin z}{z^{38}}dz∫C​z38sinz​dz

Note that if we let f(ζ)=sin⁡ζf(\zeta)=\sin \zetaf(ζ)=sinζ and z=0z=0z=0 is inside the circle, then we can use General Cauchy Integral Formula for circle CrC_rCr​ to evaluate the integral.

So we have

∫Csin⁡zz38dz=2πi37!f(37)(0)=2πi37!sin⁡(37)(0)\int_{C}\frac{\sin z}{z^{38}}dz = \frac{2\pi i}{37!} f^{(37)}(0) = \frac{2\pi i}{37!} \sin ^{(37)}(0)∫C​z38sinz​dz=37!2πi​f(37)(0)=37!2πi​sin(37)(0)

Note that sin⁡(n)(0)={0,n≡0(mod4)1,n≡1(mod4)0,n≡2(mod4)−1,n≡3(mod4)\sin ^{(n)}(0)=\begin{cases} 0,& n\equiv 0 \pmod{4}\\ 1,& n\equiv 1 \pmod{4}\\ 0,& n\equiv 2 \pmod{4}\\ -1,& n\equiv 3 \pmod{4} \end{cases}sin(n)(0)=⎩⎨⎧​0,1,0,−1,​n≡0(mod4)n≡1(mod4)n≡2(mod4)n≡3(mod4)​

So we have

∫Csin⁡zz38dz=2πi37!sin⁡(37)(0)=2πi37!⋅1=2πi37!\int_{C}\frac{\sin z}{z^{38}}dz = \frac{2\pi i}{37!} \sin ^{(37)}(0) = \frac{2\pi i}{37!} \cdot 1 = \frac{2\pi i}{37!}∫C​z38sinz​dz=37!2πi​sin(37)(0)=37!2πi​⋅1=37!2πi​

Cauchy integral is a easier way to evaluate the integral.

Liouville’s Theorem

If a function fff is entire (holomorphic on C\mathbb{C}C) and bounded, then fff is constant.

Finding power series of holomorphic functions

If fff is holomorphic on a disk ∣z−z0∣<R|z-z_0|<R∣z−z0​∣<R, then fff can be represented as a power series on the disk.

where an=f(n)(z0)n!a_n=\frac{f^{(n)}(z_0)}{n!}an​=n!f(n)(z0​)​

Example:

If q(z)=(z−1)(z−2)(z−3)q(z)=(z-1)(z-2)(z-3)q(z)=(z−1)(z−2)(z−3), find the power series of q(z)q(z)q(z) centered at z=0z=0z=0.

Note that q(z)q(z)q(z) is holomorphic on C\mathbb{C}C and q(z)=0q(z)=0q(z)=0 at z=1,2,3z=1,2,3z=1,2,3.

So we can use the power series of q(z)q(z)q(z) centered at z=1z=1z=1.

To solve this, we can simply expand q(z)=(z−1)(z−2)(z−3)q(z)=(z-1)(z-2)(z-3)q(z)=(z−1)(z−2)(z−3) and get q(z)=z3−6z2+11z−6q(z)=z^3-6z^2+11z-6q(z)=z3−6z2+11z−6.

So we have a0=q(1)=−6a_0=q(1)=-6a0​=q(1)=−6, a1=q′(1)=3z2−12z+11=11a_1=q'(1)=3z^2-12z+11=11a1​=q′(1)=3z2−12z+11=11, a2=q′′(1)2!=6z−122=−3a_2=\frac{q''(1)}{2!}=\frac{6z-12}{2}=-3a2​=2!q′′(1)​=26z−12​=−3, a3=q′′′(1)3!=66=1a_3=\frac{q'''(1)}{3!}=\frac{6}{6}=1a3​=3!q′′′(1)​=66​=1.

So the power series of q(z)q(z)q(z) centered at z=1z=1z=1 is

q(z)=−6+11(z−1)−3(z−1)2+(z−1)3q(z)=-6+11(z-1)-3(z-1)^2+(z-1)^3q(z)=−6+11(z−1)−3(z−1)2+(z−1)3

Fundamental Theorem of Algebra

Every non-constant polynomial with complex coefficients has a root in C\mathbb{C}C.

Can be factored into linear factors:

p(z)=an(z−z1)(z−z2)⋯(z−zn)p(z)=a_n(z-z_1)(z-z_2)\cdots(z-z_n)p(z)=an​(z−z1​)(z−z2​)⋯(z−zn​)

We can treat holomorphic functions as polynomials.

fff has zero of order mmm at z0z_0z0​ if and only if f(z)=(z−z0)mg(z)f(z)=(z-z_0)^m g(z)f(z)=(z−z0​)mg(z) for some holomorphic g(z)g(z)g(z) and g(z0)≠0g(z_0)\neq 0g(z0​)=0.

Zeros of holomorphic functions

If fff is holomorphic on a disk ∣z−z0∣<R|z-z_0|<R∣z−z0​∣<R and fff has a zero of order mmm at z0z_0z0​, then f(z0)=0f(z_0)=0f(z0​)=0, f′(z0)=0f'(z_0)=0f′(z0​)=0, f′′(z0)=0f''(z_0)=0f′′(z0​)=0, ⋯\cdots⋯, f(m−1)(z0)=0f^{(m-1)}(z_0)=0f(m−1)(z0​)=0 and f(m)(z0)≠0f^{(m)}(z_0)\neq 0f(m)(z0​)=0.

And there exists a holomorphic function ggg on the disk such that f(z)=(z−z0)mg(z)f(z)=(z-z_0)^m g(z)f(z)=(z−z0​)mg(z) and g(z0)≠0g(z_0)\neq 0g(z0​)=0.

Example:

Find zeros of f(z)=cos⁡(zπ2)f(z)=\cos(z\frac{\pi}{2})f(z)=cos(z2π​)

Note that f(z)=0f(z)=0f(z)=0 if and only if zπ2=(2k+1)π2z\frac{\pi}{2}=(2k+1)\frac{\pi}{2}z2π​=(2k+1)2π​ for some integer kkk.

So the zeros of f(z)f(z)f(z) are z=(2k+1)z=(2k+1)z=(2k+1) for some integer kkk.

The order of the zero is 111 since f′(z)=−π2sin⁡(zπ2)f'(z)=-\frac{\pi}{2}\sin(z\frac{\pi}{2})f′(z)=−2π​sin(z2π​) and f′(z)≠0f'(z)\neq 0f′(z)=0 for all z=(2k+1)z=(2k+1)z=(2k+1).

If fff vanishes to infinite order at z0z_0z0​ (that is, f(z0)=f′(z0)=f′′(z0)=⋯=0f(z_0)=f'(z_0)=f''(z_0)=\cdots=0f(z0​)=f′(z0​)=f′′(z0​)=⋯=0), then f(z)≡0f(z)\equiv 0f(z)≡0 on the connected open set UUU containing z0z_0z0​.

Identity Theorem

If fff and ggg are holomorphic on a connected open set U⊂CU\subset\mathbb{C}U⊂C and f(z)=g(z)f(z)=g(z)f(z)=g(z) for all zzz in a subset of UUU that has a limit point in UUU, then f(z)=g(z)f(z)=g(z)f(z)=g(z) for all z∈Uz\in Uz∈U.

Key: consider h(z)=f(z)−g(z)h(z)=f(z)-g(z)h(z)=f(z)−g(z), prove h(z)≡0h(z)\equiv 0h(z)≡0 on UUU by applying the zero of holomorphic function.

Weierstrass Theorem

Limit of a sequence of holomorphic functions is holomorphic.

Let fnf_nfn​ be a sequence of holomorphic functions on a domain D⊂CD\subset\mathbb{C}D⊂C that converges uniformly to fff on every compact subset of DDD. Then fff is holomorphic on DDD.

Maximum Modulus Principle

If fff is a non-constant holomorphic function on a domain D⊂CD\subset\mathbb{C}D⊂C, then ∣f∣|f|∣f∣ does not attain a maximum value in DDD.

Corollary: Minimum Modulus Principle

If fff is a non-constant holomorphic function on a domain D⊂CD\subset\mathbb{C}D⊂C, then 1f\frac{1}{f}f1​ does not attain a minimum value in DDD.

Schwarz Lemma

If fff is a holomorphic function on the unit disk ∣z∣<1|z|<1∣z∣<1 and ∣f(z)∣≤∣z∣|f(z)|\leq |z|∣f(z)∣≤∣z∣, then ∣f′(0)∣≤1|f'(0)|\leq 1∣f′(0)∣≤1.
